segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with an upbeat, adventurous orchestral score accompanying sweeping aerial and ground-level shots of the luxurious Belmond Andean Explorer train, painted in dark blue and cream with arched windows. A narrator, in an enthusiastic, professional male voice, welcomes viewers aboard "South America's most luxurious overnight train," highlighting its journey across Peru, "breathtaking views," and "five-star service." The train is seen traversing rugged Andean landscapes, including mountainous terrain, grassy plains, and rocky valleys, crossing bridges and winding through scenic routes, with text overlays confirming it reaches 14,000 feet (4,328 meters). The narrator emphasizes it's "one of the highest trains in the world" and "one of the most luxurious," offering "plush accommodations, craft cocktails, and over-the-top meals." Inside, the train's opulent interior features plush seating, elegant decor, and large windows. The scene transitions to Jeb Brooks, a man with a beard and plaid shirt, in a selfie-style shot from the train’s observation car, speaking directly to the camera with a mountainous backdrop. He introduces himself in a friendly, direct voice as "Jeb Brooks from GreenerGrass.com," confirming he's "on board the Andean Explorer" and inviting viewers to join him and his wife Suzanne to discover Peru's Highlands in "the absolute lap of luxury," as train rattling and a horn sound are audible. The perspective then shifts to an aerial view from an airplane window, revealing lush green valleys and the sprawling city of Cusco nestled among hills, with text overlay confirming its elevation at 11,152 feet (3,400 meters). A second, calmer male narrator explains they "flew into the old Incan capital of Cusco" the day before the train's departure. The video concludes with a ground-level view of Cusco’s historic terracotta rooftops and colonial architecture, including a prominent church dome, under a clear blue sky, as the narrator mentions they will "leave this bustling city behind" tomorrow.

The scene shifts to the Perurail train station, showing health prevention guidelines, as the "absolutely beautiful" Belmond Andean Explorer train, with its dark blue and cream carriages, arrives at the platform. A traveler with a suitcase walks towards the train, and the camera pans along its length, revealing 'BELMOND ANDEAN EXPLORER' branding and a pixelated Inca symbol. On the platform, a small band of musicians in traditional attire performs with guitar, drums, and flute, their festive, instrumental Andean music filling the air. The narrator notes the "festive atmosphere" among the 60 guests, revealing this is the train's "first official trip in more than two years." The segment concludes with two glasses of a frothy, reddish beverage, which the narrator identifies as a fermented fruit drink offered to celebrate the journey. A man and Suzanne from GreenerGrass.com toast with these drinks in front of the train, accompanied by a "cheers" and the distinct sound of clinking glasses.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens at the Perurail Train Station in Cusco, where Traditional Musicians in colorful striped shawls perform lively music on a drum, guitar, and flute. Jeb Brooks, standing beside the luxurious Belmond Andean Explorer train with its distinctive Pixelated Inca Symbol, enthusiastically declares, "Here's to a great rail journey!" He marvels at the train's impressive size, noting, "Must be something like 15 or 20 cars in this train. This thing's huge! And we just learned it's full. So, this will be a pretty... pretty exciting adventure." The vibrant traditional music continues as dancers in red and orange attire perform on the platform, followed by a couple dancing casually. Jeb's voiceover adds, "That sense of adventure built even more as dancers joined the musicians," and then, "And we all know what came next. Suzanne, of course, joined the fun." Jeb and Suzanne, wearing face masks, are then seen walking towards and boarding the train with other passengers. Inside, the elegant interior with plush seating and decorative lighting is revealed as passengers move through the corridor. Jeb's voice expresses his excitement about boarding, stating, "This is always an exciting moment when you're getting on a train and uh, this guarantees to be one of our most epic journeys yet." He explains the boarding process, mentioning that he and Suzanne were among "the lucky half" who "got to go to the observation car." The camera follows them to the open-air observation deck with ornate metal railings, and Jeb's voice continues, "Now, of course, we headed straight outside to the very back, where we found these two seats. And it was at this point, I wasn't sure we'd ever move from this spot right here." The segment concludes with Jeb and Suzanne smiling at the camera from inside the train, the scenic Andean landscape visible through the windows, as their journey begins.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with Jeb Brooks and Suzanne, dressed casually, smiling at the camera on a train platform, with the Perurail Train Station visible in the background. Jeb excitedly announces, "We're on board, outside. We're about to leave the station, outside. How crazy is that?" as Suzanne makes a happy, almost giggling sound, setting a joyful travel mood. The scene transitions to a close-up of a champagne flute on a table beside a clock, as Jeb, now wearing sunglasses, speaks to the camera, mentioning "another toast" after "24 months." The elegant dark blue and cream carriages of the Belmond Andean Explorer train, with arched windows, begin to move, pulling out of what Jeb identifies as "Huanchoc station." As the train glides through a scenic landscape, Jeb enthusiastically declares, "Only about six minutes late, but who cares? This is an adventure!" He narrates that "Our two engines slowly eased the 20 cars of this train out of Cusco," as it passes colorful buildings and winding roads alongside a highway, with lush green mountains and snow-capped peaks in the distance under a bright blue sky. The camera captures the train’s external movement, curving along tracks near a '400 Meters' sign. Almost immediately, the incredible scenery that Jeb mentions they would experience over the next three days appears, confirming that Peru offers some of the world's most beautiful countryside. As the breathtaking Andean environment continues to unfold, Jeb encourages viewers to subscribe to the channel and hit the bell icon for more transportation experiences. The video then shifts to the interior of the train, showcasing a luxurious cabin with a neatly made bed, patterned pillows, a blue armchair, and warm lighting, as Jeb realizes they "hadn't even seen our room yet!" He describes the "1300-foot-long train" as including "35 cabins divided into three types of accommodation: bunk rooms, twin rooms (which is what we'd booked), and these absolutely over-the-top suites." The final frames highlight a spacious observation deck with ornate black iron railings and wooden flooring, offering panoramic views of the passing scenery, emphasizing the train’s opulent design and the breathtaking Andean environment.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a smooth forward-moving shot down the elegant, cream-colored corridor of the Belmond Andean Explorer train, its ornate paneling and large windows revealing a passing green landscape, accompanied by gentle instrumental background music. Jeb Brooks's clear, informative voice explains that all rooms have en-suite bathrooms with showers and that each car is named for a local element. The camera focuses on a 'Coche – Car Totora' sign, describing the Uru people and their reed boats, as Jeb clarifies that 'Totora' refers to the reeds seen around Lake Titicaca. A hand reaches out to touch the door frame as the camera enters a luxurious cabin, featuring a plush gray and cream upholstered seat, a small table, and a window. A woman with dark curly hair, Suzanne, wearing a floral blouse, smiles warmly and enthusiastically welcomes the viewer, declaring, "Spoiler alert, it's incredible!" Jeb, with a beard and plaid shirt, then appears, enthusiastically explaining the cabin's features, noting it's "a lot different than an Amtrak room" and "spectacular." He details how the sofa and seat convert into twin beds at night. The camera pans to show a neatly folded bed, a storage compartment with extra pillows labeled 'ALMOHADA / PILLOW,' and a small safe with a keypad, as Jeb mentions plenty of extra towels, pillows, sheets, blankets, and plugs everywhere. He adds that the closet contains robes, a lock box, and even slippers. Finally, the bathroom is revealed, featuring a white toilet, a sink with chrome fixtures, a mirror, and a shelf with toiletries including a soap bar, a small bottle, and an 'H2O' pouch. Jeb describes it as "small but mighty," boasting "more amenities than you'd expect on a train."

segments.integrated_caption: The minute opens with a view down the elegant Train Hallway of the Belmond Andean Explorer, featuring cream-colored paneling and large windows, as a person in a blue shirt walks ahead, while gentle Latin-inspired instrumental music plays and a male narrator advises learning Spanish words like 'permiso' for such 'narrow hallways.' The scene briefly transitions to an exterior shot of a white 'ELDORADO' bus driving past a 'MULTISERVICIOS EM' station amidst green hills, before returning to the train's Observation Deck, showcasing ornate black iron railings and a panoramic mountainous landscape with lush green valleys. Jeb Brooks, in a plaid shirt, appears on screen, smiling and gesturing as he speaks with a clear American accent, noting they've started their climb and are at 'about 10,600' feet, expecting to reach 'about 14,000 and change,' and mentioning the availability of oxygen and a nurse on the train, as train sounds rumble beneath his voice. The train is then shown from a distance, winding through the Andes at ~4,328 meters with terraced fields, before revealing the luxurious cabin interior with a plush gray sofa, colorful pillows, a glass-top coffee table, and a well-stocked bar. The narrator's voice returns over the continuing music, describing the 'lounge space inside the observation car' with a 'fully stocked bar' where 'everything you eat and drink on board is included,' and highlighting the 'well-appointed bathroom' with amenities like 'JABON SOAP' and 'CREMA DE MANOS' alongside pink lilies. The segment concludes with a view of the cozy cabin seating area and bar, under warm lighting, overlooking the passing scenery.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a serene close-up of vibrant orange lilies in a terracotta vase on a moving train's windowsill, their reflection blurred in the glass, as the luxurious Belmond Andean Explorer travels through a stunning mountainous landscape with a rushing river and lush greenery visible through the large window. Amidst background music and the gentle sounds of the moving train, Jeb Brooks, a man with a beard and plaid shirt, excitedly explains that after being in the observation car, he and Suzanne received an unexpected upgrade to "the biggest room on this train," attributing it to pure luck. He describes their previous twin room and then, with increasing enthusiasm, gestures around the newly acquired, expansive suite, which features cream-colored paneling, elegant molding, a plush bed with striped pillows, and blue leather armchairs. Jeb points to a floor plan display on the wall, illustrating how "this whole thing" is now their room. Suzanne, with dark curly hair and a floral blouse, appears equally excited, waving and gesturing, her voice heard counting "nine pieces" as Jeb announces "room tour number two!" He highlights the double bed, calling it "incredible," before the camera briefly shows the well-appointed bathroom with its decorative mirror, toiletries, and a small plant. The segment concludes with Jeb and Suzanne playfully celebrating their upgrade by lying on the plush bed, raising their arms in triumph, as the train continues its scenic journey through the Andes.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with an upbeat, folksy instrumental background music as the camera showcases the luxurious interior of a Belmond Andean Explorer cabin, featuring a neatly made bed, two blue leather armchairs, a small table, and a dark wooden dresser. Jeb Brooks, a man with a beard and plaid shirt, appears, smiling and speaking directly to the camera in a clear, narrative male voice, explaining the amenities like the movable chairs, dresser, and table. He highlights an "added benefit" of the room: the ability to open a window to see the other side of the train. As he exclaims, "Look at this," Suzanne, a woman with dark curly hair, is seen opening a small emergency exit window in the cabin door, revealing the stunning Andean landscape outside. Jeb expresses his awe, declaring, "This is incredible!" The scene transitions to sweeping views of golden fields, rolling hills, and grazing sheep as the train moves through the picturesque countryside. Jeb's narration continues, explaining that despite their "residual shock," it was time to head to one of the train's two restaurant cars. Due to the train being full, passengers were divided into two groups, and they were assigned the second seating, meaning they would eat after 2 PM. Inside the elegant dining area, a table is set for two with brown leather chairs and a vase of flowers. Jeb details that passengers are presented with menus offering an appetizer or two and two main course choices. A close-up of the Belmond Andean Explorer lunch menu lists gourmet dishes like 'Giant corn and cheese' and 'Seared sea bass with muña-scented broad beans.' Jeb confirms that they both selected the seared sea bass. Beautifully plated dishes, including the seared sea bass with garnishes and sauces, are shown as the train continues its journey through the Andes. Jeb reflects on the experience, stating he wasn't sure whether to watch the "incredibly beautiful" scenery or the food, concluding that the food was "five-star all the way." He adds that the chefs work both on the trains and in Belmond's hotels around Peru, ensuring quality despite the "cramped quarters." The final shots include a hillside with a large 'CHANITO' sign and a close-up of the seared sea bass dish.

The video then shows the actual plated dishes, beautifully presented with garnishes and sauces, while the train continues its journey through the picturesque Andes. The final shots include a hillside with a large 'CHANITO' sign and a close-up of the seared sea bass dish, reinforcing the luxurious and scenic nature of the train journey.

The man in the plaid shirt explains that "Laraya" is a place to "stretch our legs" and "check out the local church," and mentions a "small market" where "Suzanne bought some gloves" that would "come in handy" as they continued their journey.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with Suzanne, a woman with dark curly hair, wearing a black jacket and yellow-lensed sunglasses, standing before a small white chapel with a red-tiled roof, speaking to the camera about a purchase she made: "my new gloves." The scene transitions to a bustling outdoor market set against a dramatic mountain range, where on-screen text highlights her concern about altitude, a sentiment Suzanne confirms, stating, "altitude was one of my big concerns coming on this trip." The luxurious Belmond Andean Explorer train is then shown traveling through the rugged Andean landscape, with on-screen text indicating they are at the highest point of the journey. Suzanne's voice confirms this, saying, "And here we are at the highest point of the train journey." She is then seen with the Man in Plaid Shirt, both wearing sunglasses, discussing their altitude experience. Suzanne explains, "I'll say I'm feeling 100%, but I'm not doing too bad," attributing her management to prescribed altitude medicine, hydration, and, as on-screen text humorously adds, "Well, plus some Wine and Gin & Tonics... 😳." The man, with a beard, wearing a plaid shirt and black sunglasses, adds his perspective, stating, "Like I'm not ready to run a marathon, that's for sure, but generally, you know, it's not that bad." A male narrator then interjects, explaining, "But our time in Laraya was coming to a close. So it was time to get back on the train and head out." Suzanne is shown boarding the train, followed by sweeping views of the mountainous terrain, including misty peaks, grazing alpacas, and a winding river, all set to atmospheric background music. The narrator continues, "And it was here, as we left Laraya behind and the moon rose while the sun set, that the scenery truly became extraordinary. This was just one of those moments where a camera hardly does it justice." The video concludes with serene shots of train tracks stretching into the distance, the sun setting behind the mountains, and a river flowing through the valley, emphasizing the vast beauty of the Andes, accompanied by the continuing music.

The camera cuts to Suzanne and the Man in Plaid Shirt standing together, both wearing sunglasses, as they discuss their altitude experience—Suzanne notes she’s not feeling 100% but is managing, attributing it to prescribed medicine and some wine and gin & tonics. The man adds that he’s not ready to run a marathon but is generally okay. The video then shows Suzanne boarding the train, followed by sweeping views of the mountainous terrain, including misty peaks, grazing alpacas, and a winding river. The final shots capture the train tracks stretching into the distance, the sun setting behind the mountains, and a serene river flowing through the valley, emphasizing the vast, remote beauty of the Andes.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a serene wide shot of a clear blue sky, where a small flock of birds flies in formation above a rugged mountain range, accompanied by gentle, acoustic background music. The scene transitions to a sweeping view from the train, showcasing a winding river cutting through golden grasslands with distant snow-capped peaks under a soft sunset glow. The camera then captures the train moving along a track through a valley, with a river flowing below and rolling hills in the background, bathed in warm golden light. A lone cyclist rides along a road parallel to the train tracks, silhouetted against the vast, sunlit landscape. The perspective shifts to a view from the train’s observation deck, framed by ornate iron railings, as the sun dips below the horizon, casting a vibrant orange and purple sky over a meandering river. The scene then moves to a quiet, abandoned white building in a field at dusk, before transitioning to a close-up of Suzanne and Jeb inside a luxurious train cabin. Suzanne's voice is heard stating, "So, it's time to head out for cocktails and then dinner," adding that "The dress code on this train is smart casual," which is reinforced by the on-screen text 'SMART CASUAL'. She playfully asks Jeb, "And Jeb, what did you do?" to which he responds with a laugh, and Suzanne remarks, "Always smart casual, this one over here." Jeb then narrates, expressing he felt "slightly judged for my choice of uniform" as they "made our way to the piano bar, where an onboard musician played." He highlights that "This train has its own baby grand piano," before the audio cuts off as he mentions "After cocktails and tapas..." Meanwhile, the couple shares a laugh, and the camera pans to reveal the elegant interior of the cabin with plush seating, decorative pillows, a lamp with a blue shade, and a small table with pink flowers. A hand adjusts a pillow, and the scene focuses on two gin and tonic glasses, garnished with cucumber and lime, on a dark table, suggesting the relaxed, sophisticated atmosphere of their upcoming cocktails. The gentle background music continues throughout.

segments.integrated_caption: The video begins with a close-up of a hand holding a creamy, garnished drink on a dimly lit dining table, elegantly set with wine glasses, a lit candle, and pink flowers, aboard the Belmond Andean Explorer. A male voice, identified as Jeb Brooks, narrates that they headed to one of the two restaurant cars for dinner, having pre-selected their main course, beef, from a digital menu displaying gourmet options like 'Vegetable water and caramelized cauliflower,' 'Sun-dried tomatoes, sage and pine nut tortellini,' 'Beef tenderloin with caramelized pumpkin cream and Andean cereals,' and 'Chocolate texture.' Jeb explains that the meal is inclusive, offering a choice of red or white wine. The camera then shows a woman smiling warmly at the table, followed by close-ups of the plated dishes: two small glasses of the 'spectacular' vegetable water and caramelized cauliflower, the 'perfectly prepared' beef tenderloin with caramelized pumpkin cream and Andean cereals, and finally, a chocolate texture dessert. Jeb expresses his impressiveness with the quality of the food, given the constraints of cooking on a train. The scene transitions to Jeb Brooks himself, a man with a beard and graying hair, lying in bed and speaking directly to the camera, reflecting that it's about 9:45 PM and time for 'shut-eye' before an early morning to catch the sunrise over Lake Titicaca. The video concludes with a black screen displaying 'Day 2: Puno, Peru, Altitude: 12,500 Feet (3,810 Meters),' as Jeb describes waking up at 12,500 feet on the banks of the world's highest navigable lake, mentioning that Suzanne was grateful for her gloves. This is followed by a shot of a person in a blue jacket walking down a well-lit train hallway, passing pink flowers, indicating the transition to the next day’s journey.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a serene sunrise over a calm body of water, identified by the narrator as Lake Titicaca, which he describes as "one of my favorite places on earth" and "just perfect." The sky is painted in warm hues of orange, pink, and purple as ducks glide across the reflective surface, creating gentle ripples, while distant hills and islands are silhouetted against the glowing horizon. A small boat moves swiftly, leaving a white wake, with two silhouetted figures gazing at the sunrise. The narrator expresses his deep affection for the lake, highlighting its "so much history and meaning" as the "birthplace for the Incas and the Tiahuanacu people," its "fascinating biodiversity," and its unique status as the only place where landlocked Bolivia operates its navy. He notes they "really lucked out with the weather" during their mid-April trip, seeing "not a drop" of rain. As the sun rises, casting golden light, the scene transitions to a train yard at dawn, where a blue Perurail locomotive numbered 654 is stationed on tracks. The camera then focuses on the luxurious 'BELMOND ANDEAN EXPLORER' train, with cream and navy blue carriages, positioned on a loading platform beside the water, with a crane in the background, suggesting preparation for departure. The narrator explains that once the sun was up, they'd head back on board for breakfast before "leaving the train once more for a trip to one of South America's most unusual neighborhoods." He clarifies that "this is not a traditional train trip" but rather one with "built-in excursions" designed to give passengers unique experiences. Throughout this peaceful and anticipatory atmosphere, a gentle, ambient background music plays, accompanying the narrator's reflective commentary.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a wide shot of a blue and yellow Perurail locomotive, numbered 654, stationed at a train yard under a clear morning sky, accompanied by gentle, uplifting background music. A man, wearing a cap with a British flag patch and sunglasses, narrates, introducing the journey as a chance to see 'a slice of Peru.' The scene transitions to a serene time-lapse of a golden sunrise over a calm lake, with boats gently moving, which the man describes as 'one of the most beautiful sunrises' he's ever seen, specifically at 'Lake Titicaca.' He then reappears, speaking directly to the camera with the locomotive behind him, before being joined by a woman in a colorful knit beanie. As they walk along the platform beside the train, the woman asks about his sleep, to which he replies he 'slept really well,' attributing it to the altitude, and she confirms it was 'a good night.' The camera then captures a close-up of a plaque on a train carriage, reading 'DESIGNED BY QUEENSLAND RAIL'S ROLLING STOCK ENGINEERS AND MANUFACTURED BY QUEENSLAND RAIL'S TOOWOOMBA WORKSHOPS.' The man explains that the train's cars were 'refurbished from Australia' and that the plaque 'highlights that,' asking the woman if it makes her want to return to Australia for a train trip. She enthusiastically agrees, stating it's 'on the list,' prompting him to suggest specific Australian trains like the 'Indian Pacific' or 'Ghan.' The scene shifts to a dining table set for breakfast with small bowls of jam, butter, and a cup of tea, as the man announces it was 'time for breakfast.' The final shot shows a brown dog walking along the gravel beside the train tracks, which the man humorously refers to as 'at least one local' observing the scene.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a breathtaking panoramic view of Lake Titicaca under a clear blue sky, showcasing floating islands made of totora reeds with traditional reed houses and colorful accents. A narrator explains that this is the world's highest navigable lake at 12,500 feet above sea level, and South America's largest, with the reed islands described as floating reeds woven together, offering a safe refuge for the people who call them home for at least 4,000 years. A motorized boat labeled 'WILLYBEBE' glides across the water near these islands, followed by a small white motorboat with two individuals, one in a red skirt and straw hat, navigating through the reeds. The scene transitions to a close-up of a large, ornate yellow and red reed boat with decorative animal heads, being paddled by a person in a life vest. With traditional music playing in the background, the narrator humorously notes that if you disagree with a neighbor on these islands, you can "just cut 'em off." The camera then moves to a first-person perspective, showing feet walking on the textured reed-covered surface of a floating island, followed by a woman in a green vest, blue skirt, and straw hat, carrying a bundle of reeds. The narrator recounts jumping at the opportunity to travel in one of these traditional reed boats, even getting to row, noting its difficulty at nearly three miles above sea level, as the view from the reed boat shows the water and distant islands. Another reed boat with passengers, including a man in a hat and a woman in a black jacket, is seen being paddled by a man in a life vest. The final scenes offer a wide view of the lake with a small motorboat and a reed boat in the distance, set against hills and mountains under a bright blue sky, as the narrator reflects on how seeing these islands and meeting their people offered a reminder that their way of life is just one way humans have made the world home, concluding that their brief stay offered valuable perspective.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a serene view of Lake Titicaca, its deep blue waters under a clear sky, featuring floating reed islands and traditional reed houses. A small motorized boat glides across the lake, while a yellow inflatable raft floats nearby. A man with a beard and graying hair, wearing a gray quilted jacket and a cap with a British flag patch, speaks directly to the camera in a British accent, explaining that unlike traditional rail journeys, their train allows passengers to get out and explore, noting it hasn't moved since 11 pm last night and they aren't scheduled to leave until after lunch, emphasizing the trip is designed to experience these parts of the world. He mentions leaving the floating islands behind to return to the train, adding, "Or so we thought." The scene transitions to a Peruvian flag waving in the wind, with reed houses and a water tower in the background, as flute-like music plays. The scene shifts to a train station where the same man, now wearing sunglasses, stands beside a woman with curly hair and a plaid shirt. The woman, speaking in an American accent, exclaims, "No train! Where'd it go?" The man responds, "I don't know, but uh... It was here this morning. Our train was gone." The woman adds, "This is really weird." She then clarifies they aren't scheduled to depart for a few hours, so she's "not so worried about that." The man explains that something on the train broke, and it was sent away to be fixed, adding that nobody on staff seemed too concerned, as the plan all along was to have a buffet lunch at the private station Belmond maintains in Puno. They walk along the train tracks, with a blue Perurail locomotive visible in the distance. The video then shows an outdoor dining setup by the water, with white tables and chairs arranged near a large rusted crane, followed by a close-up of a beautifully set table with colorful glassware and cutlery, confirming the buffet lunch. The final scenes depict a buffet with a hand serving a plate of rice, vegetables, and greens, and the couple smiling at the camera again, with the train station and mountains in the background, as the woman notes it's "almost two hours since we got back."

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of a man and a woman, both wearing caps and sunglasses, standing beside train tracks under a bright blue sky. The woman, in a plaid shirt and a Delta-branded cap, speaks about their "excursion on the lake," noting "still no train, but we did have lunch." The man, wearing a gray shirt and a cap with a British flag patch, then speculates, "we think we might have heard a train horn," suggesting "it's possible the train's coming in now." The woman adds, "Stand by," as gentle, melodic music begins to play. The scene shifts to a wide shot of a blue locomotive numbered 751 approaching on the tracks, with a worker in an orange vest nearby. The camera then pans to a luxurious train carriage labeled 'BELMOND ANDEAN EXPLORER' in navy blue and cream, parked on the tracks. The couple reappears, now standing in front of the train, smiling and talking. The woman exclaims, "It's back. That's reassuring," to which the man playfully asks, "when I said it was coming back, did you doubt me a little bit?" The woman replies, "I like it. I like the feeling of it being here a lot better." The man's voice transitions to a narration, stating, "Despite that hiccup, we were still on schedule. The plan was to stay here for a little while longer, so I decided to get cleaned up." The view transitions to a window shot from inside the train, showing a serene lake and distant hills under a clear sky. The scene cuts to the man inside a well-appointed train cabin, pointing to the shower area. He speaks directly to the camera, asking, "You know what this is? This is a guy who needs a shower. And thankfully, this incredible room has an incredible shower." He adds, "I'm gonna hop in there and hopefully look a little different in just a second." The camera shows the decorative showerhead running with the distinct sound of water, then returns to the man, now in a plaid shirt, speaking directly to the camera in the cabin. He reports, "Good news, I feel a lot better. Bad news, the shower won't stop running. They're bringing the mechanics on uh to try to fix it." The lighting is warm and natural, emphasizing the luxurious and comfortable interior of the train cabin, as the man concludes with a narration, "As a reminder, this is the first time..."

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a bustling Peruvian street scene, featuring a white van labeled '007' and a white car navigating through traffic amidst pedestrians and vendors, all set to upbeat, light instrumental music. The scene transitions to a nighttime view from a moving train, revealing a dark landscape with distant city lights reflecting on water and mountains under a twilight sky, as the train travels along tracks through rugged, dimly lit terrain with scattered buildings and debris. Jeb Brooks' voiceover begins, stating, "With the sun below the horizon, we headed back to the piano bar before dinner." A close-up of the 'Belmond Andean Explorer's' digital menu is shown, listing gourmet dishes like 'Lima Bean Cappuccino,' 'Ocopa with Mushrooms,' and 'Duck Ravioli,' while Jeb Brooks' voiceover declares, "Coming as no surprise at all, tonight's meal was superb." A woman is then shown sipping from a small black cup labeled 'Lima Bean Cappuccino,' which Jeb Brooks identifies as "Suzanne's highlight." Close-ups of plated dishes, including 'Ocopa with Mushrooms' and 'Duck Ravioli with Seared Breast, Sweet Potatoes, Nuts and Kañiwa,' are presented as Jeb Brooks remarks that "she just couldn't get enough of it." He continues, expressing his enthusiasm, "And I was over the moon for the duck, which I'd chosen as my main course." The final frames show Jeb Brooks speaking directly to the camera in a train cabin, concluding, "That's a wrap on day two. We've just reached Saracocha and we're about 14,000 feet. It's about 10 o'clock. In other words, it's time for bed. See you in the morning." This is followed by a black screen with white on-screen text reading 'Day 3: Saracocha, Peru, Altitude: 14,000 Feet (4,267 Meters).' After a brief pause, Jeb Brooks' voice returns, remarking, "That was a pretty good night's sleep."

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a breathtaking sunrise over a vast, arid landscape, where the cream and navy blue Belmond Andean Explorer train snakes along a valley beside a calm lake. A male narrator recounts, "We made our way back down the hill and onto the train," where they found "hot coffee in the observation car," just as the train pulled away from this "picturesque setting" to reveal "some of the most stunning scenery we'd seen yet." Gentle, uplifting background music accompanies the journey. From a first-person perspective, the tracks curve through rugged terrain with distant mountains under a clear blue sky. A lone railway worker is then seen adjusting a railway switch near a red-and-white signal, a necessary action as the narrator explains, "the switch needed to be changed for the mining trains that also use this track." The train, identified by the narrator as the "Andean Explorer," continues its graceful movement through the high-altitude Andean countryside, winding past grassy hills and rocky outcrops. An interior shot briefly showcases a luxurious dining table set with pink flowers, food, and champagne, reflecting the elegant experience. The narrator provides context, stating that "Belmond operates luxury hotels and trains throughout the world," and that this "scenery along through here almost made us feel like we were aboard that train through the Scottish Highlands." He further details that the "Andean Explorer began in 2017 as South America's first luxury sleeper train," a 50-50 joint venture between Belmond and Peru Rail. Captured from multiple angles, including its observation deck, the train offers expansive views of the lake and surrounding mountains. The final shots show the train ascending a steep hillside under a brilliant blue sky, with the vast lake and distant peaks below, as the narrator emphasizes that "These tracks have been vital to Peru's transportation network for a long time," underscoring the route's adventure and isolation.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with sweeping aerial shots of the luxurious Belmond Andean Explorer train, a cream and navy blue marvel labeled 'BELMOND ANDEAN EXPLORER,' as it snakes along a rugged, arid mountainous landscape under a clear blue sky. The narrator's voice immediately praises South America as their "favorite continent," emphasizing that "this train" offers "tremendous opportunities for exploration and discovery on this magnificent continent." The camera pans across the expansive Andean scenery, revealing vast, undulating hills, rocky outcrops, and a serene, deep-blue lake, likely Lake Titicaca, nestled in the valley below. The sun shines brightly, casting sharp shadows and highlighting the golden-brown grasses. A first-person view from the train looks down the tracks, emphasizing the journey’s isolation and grandeur, accompanied by gentle, reflective background music. Inside the train, a beautifully set breakfast table appears, featuring a white place mat with 'ANDEAN EXPLORER' printed on it, a fluffy vegetable omelette, fresh avocado, crispy bacon, toast, orange juice, coffee, and small bowls of jam and butter. The narrator explains, "Our stomachs brought us back to reality and we made our way forward for breakfast," adding that "delicious as the food was, and it was always delicious." The scene transitions back to exterior shots of the train moving through the high-altitude landscape, with the lake and distant snow-capped peaks visible. The narrator reflects on the "seemingly endless horseshoe curves" being "far more enthralling" than the food, and how it's "impossible not to daydream while taking in this landscape." He continues, noting that Peru, and much of South America, "has been a cradle of civilization for thousands of years," even discussing the relatively short-lived Inca Empire. The final frames return to the interior, showing a close-up of the breakfast table with a hand reaching for bread, reinforcing the luxurious and serene experience of dining while traveling through this breathtaking terrain, all underscored by the continuous, gentle music.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a sweeping view of a rugged, arid Andean landscape, characterized by rolling hills and sparse vegetation under a clear blue sky. A male narrator begins to speak, detailing how ancient civilizations embraced incredible technologies like advanced architecture, deeply meaningful art, and irrigation. As the long, cream and navy blue Belmond Andean Explorer train snakes steadily through this high-altitude terrain, curving along tracks past small pools and rocky outcrops, the narrator highlights the Tiahuanaco people's ability to produce 21 metric tons of potatoes per acre in this region, contrasting it with modern techniques that yield only 14 metric tons. Gentle, melodic background music accompanies the serene scene as the train continues its journey through expansive plains with distant snow-capped mountains, while the narrator reflects on how much knowledge has been lost over time. The camera captures grazing alpacas, their light brown and white coats contrasting with the dry grass. The narrator then advises keeping a sharp eye out for wild vicuñas, noting that unlike their cousins, llamas and alpacas, these beautiful animals cannot be domesticated, and their wool was so fine that only Incas royals could wear it. A man with graying hair and a beard is seen leaning out of the train’s observation deck, gazing at the vast, isolated natural beauty of the Andes, as the train smoothly curves around hills and crosses open fields under the bright, clear sky.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a wide shot of a vast, arid landscape where a small herd of vicuñas grazes peacefully under a clear blue sky, with distant snow-capped mountains forming a dramatic backdrop, accompanied by gentle, orchestral music. The scene transitions to the elegant cream-colored hallway of the Belmond Andean Explorer train, where Jeb Brooks, wearing a dark jacket and a cap with a colorful patch, speaks enthusiastically to the camera, explaining, "We're heading up to the piano bar because we've got another excursion, the last one of the trip. We're going to go explore some caves near the near the tracks here," as the gentle orchestral music continues and the train's rumbling is subtly heard. The camera then pans to a luxurious lounge car featuring plush gray sofas, a black grand piano, and ornate wall art, emphasizing the train’s opulence, while the orchestral music swells. Outside, the train continues through the rugged terrain, capturing a distant volcano emitting a plume of smoke against the mountain range. Jeb Brooks' narration overlays the scene, stating, "Spotting this volcanic eruption was a reminder of just how volatile this part of our planet can be. Thankfully, our caving experience wouldn't go too deep underground," as the train's exterior, prominently displaying 'BELMOND ANDEAN EXPLORER,' is shown moving through rocky canyons and arid hills, with the orchestral music still playing. Jeb Brooks then exclaims, "Time to go spelunking!" as the music transitions to a more adventurous, slightly percussive tune. The scene shifts to the Cueva de Sumbay, marked by a black sign with white lettering, where a person walks along a narrow rocky path, exploring ancient rock art. Jeb Brooks narrates, "A short hike down into this canyon introduced us to cave paintings dating back more than 10,000 years. Another reminder of just how ancient this part of the world is," accompanying close-ups of faint petroglyphs on the rock face. The video concludes with a selfie-style shot of Jeb Brooks and a woman, Suzanne, smiling together against the canyon backdrop. Suzanne, wearing a black Columbia jacket and sunglasses, humorously advises, "Never trust a hike that goes down first," to which Jeb Brooks replies, "That's good advice, probably for life too," as the adventurous music continues. The train then pulls away from the stop, with the sounds of its departure fading into the continuing music.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with sweeping aerial and ground-level shots of a rugged, arid Andean landscape, showcasing expansive rocky terrain, sparse vegetation, and towering snow-capped mountains under a clear blue sky. A gentle, orchestral travel-themed music begins as a male narrator reflects on past experiences, mentioning being "chased out" and a dog's story. The Belmond Andean Explorer train, with its cream and navy blue carriages, is seen traversing this remote terrain, crossing a green metal bridge over a deep ravine and snaking along narrow tracks carved through rocky hillsides. The camera alternates between wide panoramic views of the desert-like valleys and close-ups of the train tracks, emphasizing the isolation and grandeur of the environment. A majestic volcano dominates the horizon in several frames, while a bird of prey soars across the sky. The narrator describes the diverse passengers on board, including families, groups, honeymooners, and solo travelers, mostly from South America, Europe, or North America, noting the friendly atmosphere where conversations easily strike up over a Pisco Sour in the observation car. He adds that they never had to share a table during meals and that the experience wasn't "stuffy," with a range of clothing from smart casual to jeans, which he himself wore to dinner. The perspective shifts to a first-person view from the front of the train, looking down the tracks as they curve through the landscape, where a herd of vicuñas grazes peacefully in the distance. The scene transitions to the interior of the train, where the man with a beard and a cap featuring a British flag patch, who is the narrator, speaks directly to the camera from the doorway, with the tracks and desert stretching behind him. He explains that the train stops near Colca Canyon, where Belmond has a hotel, and that many passengers take the train from Cusco to stay there. He concludes by stating they are continuing on to Arequipa, the train's terminus. The final shot reveals the luxurious lounge car interior, featuring elegant cream paneling, a bar with bottles, patterned cushions, and a large window framing the distant snow-capped peaks, blending opulence with the raw beauty of the Andes.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of a gourmet dish, 'Sucking pig, apple and vanilla, caramelized turnips and mustard sauce,' presented on a white plate, as a calm male narrator begins, reflecting on the trip: "Despite the slight hiccups in the second day, we couldn't have asked for a better experience." Sweeping aerial views of lush green valleys and rugged, arid landscapes follow, accompanied by the narrator's continued praise: "Every single aspect of this trip was incredible." A blue and cream Perurail train, marked 'PERURAIL,' then winds through the dry terrain, passing a yellow pickup truck, while the narrator emphasizes the unique quality of the experience. The camera shifts to a first-person view of train tracks curving through a desert-like environment with cacti, leading to a panoramic view of a distant town. The narrator highlights what made the trip special: "But what put it over the top, what made it so special, were of course, the people working on board." Scenes of rural life unfold, showing a person in an orange helmet on a rocky slope, a woman in a red skirt tending to a cow in a grassy field, and a group of people walking along a roadside. The narrator details the crew: "There were 38 crew members on board to ensure the comfort of 60 passengers." The Perurail train reappears, crossing a green metal bridge over a valley with a town below, as the narrator praises the "warmth of the Peruvian people" who "waved enthusiastically as the train went by," noting that "this train is a point of pride" and expressing honor in being part of its return to glory. He concludes by stating, "This train quickly became home for us and we cannot wait to get back on board a Belmond train sometime soon." The video ends with a man in a plaid shirt and cap smiling at the camera against a mountainous backdrop, as the narrator delivers a final farewell, "Between now and the next time, see you," followed by a distinct train horn sound and a 'BLOOPERS' title card.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a scenic shot of train tracks stretching into a mountainous landscape at sunrise, overlaid with the word 'BLOOPERS' in bold white text, setting a lighthearted, behind-the-scenes tone, as the distinct sound of train rails is heard and a male voice says "rails." Upbeat, lighthearted background music begins as the scene transitions to a man in a dark jacket and cap with a British flag patch, wearing sunglasses, smiling at the camera beside a yellow and gray locomotive numbered 654. The man, speaking with a British accent, then quips, "That's a blooper." He is later seen with a woman in a floral dress, both smiling in front of a white door, suggesting a casual, friendly interaction, while the man's voice continues, explaining, "The toilet and the shower, they're two separate things." The man, now in a plaid shirt, appears on a train observation deck with a glass of gin and tonic, as a female voice asks, "Wait, what?" Inside a train cabin with a woman in a black top, the man's voice continues, "And I'm not 100% sure whether this is a prune, it could be a raisin... That was a raisin." Outdoors near train tracks with a woman in a knit beanie and sunglasses, the man reflects, "That was an early morning, but one of the most beautiful sunsets I can remember seeing." In a close-up on a train, looking out at a river, the man's voice states, "You'll have more information than we did." Later, in a white shirt, seemingly in a train hallway, looking down with a thoughtful expression, a female voice is heard saying, "Oh, excuse me." The man's voice adds, "We have to know where to look," followed by another male voice agreeing, "Yeah." He then states, "It's more complicated than people realize," to which a female voice responds, "Sure." He then addresses someone, "Suzanne, guess what?" and the female voice replies, "What?" He excitedly declares in Spanish, "El tren está aquí," then asks, "You want to be in this?" to which the female voice responds, "Uh, no." He replies, "Okay. Well," before repeating, "El tren está aquí." The female voice then humorously states, "I'm the brains of the operation. What can I say?" She asks, "What?" and he repeats for a third time, "El tren está aquí." He then directly asks, "Is that right, Suzanne?" and adds, "In a couple hours, which uh..." A train horn sounds, and he concludes by saying, "Sarah Cope." The video captures moments of joy, travel, and candid interactions, with changing backdrops from rugged landscapes to cozy train interiors, all under bright, natural lighting that emphasizes the golden hour and clear skies.
